dialogue.utterance: Medic 12, respond to a medical call at 2458 West Main Street for an adult male with chest pain. Caller reports the patient is awake and breathing.
Hello sir, we’re EMS. My name is Alex and this is my partner. We’re here to help you today.
Can you tell me your name and what’s going on today?
My chest started hurting about twenty minutes ago, and I’m feeling a little short of breath.
Sir, are you alert right now? Do you know your name, where you are, and what day it is?
Yes, my name is Michael Turner. I’m at home, and it’s Tuesday.
We'll now check your skin for injuries. Do you see any lacerations, burns, or bruises besides the one on your head?
There was a small cut there when I hit my head. Otherwise, nothing else.
We noted no other abrasions, rashes, or bruising. Your skin appears intact and you have normal skin turgor.
Let's check your head and neck. Does moving your head hurt?
Yes, turning my head increases the pain.
Your pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light. No signs of swelling or blood behind the globe.
Have you noticed any drainage from your ears, nose, or mouth?
Not that I've noticed.
We observed no signs of obstruction or visible foreign bodies in your ears or throat.
I've noticed your breathing is mild without flaring or retractions. Would you say this is normal?
Yes, this feels normal.
we heard bilateral breath sounds with no adventitious sounds. No asymmetry in chest movement noted.
Your peripheral pulse is strong and compared to central pulse. Heart sounds also seem to be regular and without added gallops.
Your tummy doesn't look distended or tender on palpation. Any stomach discomfort you want to mention?
No, everything is fine there.
I'm now checking your arms and legs. I'll avoid touching the left arm as it's weak for you-is that okay?
That's fine.
Your right arm and legs move smoothly without obvious deformity or angulation. Left arm strength remains limited.
You did not report any issues going to the bathroom. How is your urinary output lately?
It's regular.
You have no visible signs of reddish stool or blood in your urine. We don't notice any swelling or bruising.
That concludes the secondary assessment. Your overall condition is stable, with bilateral arm discomfort as the main issue.
Are you experiencing dizziness due to movement or standing?
Yes, I sometimes get dizzy when I move.

Vitals were reassessed en route. Your pulse dropped to 45, your blood pressure is 132 over 79, your breathing remains 16, and oxygen saturation rose to 94 percent. You're still experiencing sinus bradycardia on EKG.
Please let us know if anything changes during transport.
We're heading to VCU Health Systems, which is the closest appropriate facility for you.
We'll be lifting you to the stretcher using an ELK. Let's get ready.
Mr. Norman, we'll be using straps and rails to secure you for transport. Straps are now in place.
You looked at the wall during your fall, right? Any problems with moving or sitting up now?
No, I sit okay. I don't feel like I need more rest.
We're going to recheck your pulse and other vitals before we move.
The pulse during reassessment is 45 beats per minute.
Blood pressure is 132 over 79 mmHg, no significant change noted from earlier.
Your oxygen saturation is now 94%.
A second blood glucose level remains unchanged at 250.
EKG continues to show sinus bradycardia and this is consistent with your history.
How is your pain level right now?
Still a 6. No real difference from before.
Mr. Norman, is there any part of your body that feels weak?
The left arm is weak, but that has been the case for a while.
Is there anything that seems to make the pain worse?
Movement and touch seem to make it worse.
Do you have any medication currently that you use for chest pain or other discomfort?
I take Eliquis, Coreg, and Zetia, nothing specifically for pain.
Lastly, your neck pain-does it worsen when you move your head?
Yes, I feel it increase a little when I turn my head.
And your chronic back pain-does it flare up during this transport?
I manage it, but I do feel some pressure increasing.
That's noted, and we'll take care to keep you as comfortable as possible throughout the journey to the hospital.
It might get a bit bumpy moving to the truck, but we'll make it as smooth as possible.
If anything changes-whether your breathing feels off or your pain increases-please let us know right away.
All checks are done and the truck is ready. Let's get you safely to the next step of your care.
Mr. Norman, we're beginning the transfer now. Ready?
You're now on board. We should be making good time to reach VCU in about twenty minutes.
I'll monitor your condition on the way, and check in with you every few minutes.
Mr. Norman, how are you feeling now that we're moving?
Still a little uncomfortable with my arms, but not too bad.
You're holding up fine so far, and we haven't seen any worsening of your symptoms.
How about the level of discomfort in your arms-has it gone down any?
No, still the same 6.
Any changes in your breathing pattern or volume?
Still steady, no shortness of breath.
No symptoms of chest pain or dizziness reported.
Are you able to speak clearly or focused?
Yes, I feel like I can talk normally.
We identified a laceration on your head after the fall, but you remained alert and showed no signs of major injury.
No change in your condition as of now, and we'll be at VCU soon for further treatment.
Alright, we are making good progress to VCU for further care and imaging to be fully confident we caught everything.
Thank you for being cooperative with us, Mr. Norman. You did great throughout this process.
We should be arriving at the facility in approximately 12 minutes, and I'll be at the head of your stretcher when we get there.
The team at the hospital will carry over our notes and be expecting you-thank you.
We are approaching VCU Health Systems now. Getting ready for handoff to the emergency team.
We'll be unloading once the next bay is ready. Your stretcher will be transferred gently.
You can expect a full physical and neurological review once you arrive.
Thank you for using our service. The hospital is now prepared to care for you.
Have a safe drive at the hospital and get the care you need to recover fully.
If you need to reach our department or have follow-up concerns, feel free to contact us.
Goodbye, Mr. Norman. You did great today-thank you for your patience and endurance.
